title: I/O Project: Building a CLI Program

questions.question: How do you collect command-line arguments in Rust?
questions.explanation: `std::env::args()` returns an iterator over CLI arguments. `.collect()` gathers them into a `Vec<String>`. The first element is the program name.

questions.question: Separating logic into lib.rs and keeping main.rs thin is a Rust best practice for testability.
questions.explanation: Putting logic in lib.rs makes it testable as a library. main.rs handles argument parsing and calls into the library. Integration tests can only test lib.rs.

questions.question: Read the contents of a file into a String:
questions.explanation: `fs::read_to_string()` reads the entire file into a String. It returns `io::Result<String>`, so you can use `?` to propagate errors.

questions.question: Why is it better to return `Result<T, E>` from functions instead of calling `process::exit()`?
options: It allows callers to handle errors and makes the code testable
process::exit doesn't work on all platforms
questions.explanation: Returning Result lets callers decide how to handle errors. Tests can check for Err without the process actually exiting.

questions.question: Write error messages to stderr instead of stdout:
questions.explanation: `eprintln!` writes to stderr. This keeps error messages separate from normal output, which matters when piping stdout to a file.

questions.question: Test-driven development (TDD) means writing tests before writing the implementation code.
questions.explanation: TDD follows: write a failing test, implement just enough code to pass it, then refactor. The chapter demonstrates this with the search functionality.
